body: This patient is experiencing re-feeding syndrome, a potentially fatal complication of rapidly feeding a severely malnourished patient. It is characterized by severe shifts in electrolytes, most notably low phosphate (PO4), low magnesium (Mg), low calcium (Ca), and fluid retention.\

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic complaining of difficulty falling asleep. She states that as she is trying to fall asleep, she experiences uncomfortable "creepy-crawly" sensations on her legs. These sensations are only relieved when she gets out of bed and walks around the room. Medical workup reveals an underlying iron-deficiency anemia. Which of the following medications is the most appropriate treatment for her sleep-related symptoms?
questions.educationalObjective: Recognize Restless Legs Syndrome, evaluate for secondary causes like iron deficiency, and treat with dopamine agonists.\

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient's presentation of "creepy-crawly" sensations on the legs that are worse at night and relieved by movement is diagnostic of Restless Legs Syndrome (classified as Dyssomnia NOS), which is often secondary to iron-deficiency anemia or chronic kidney disease. The treatment of choice is a dopamine agonist, such as ropinirole or pramipexole.\

body: Modafinil (A) is used to treat daytime sleepiness in narcolepsy. Zolpidem (B) is a GABA-A receptor agonist used for primary insomnia, not RLS. Haloperidol (D) is a dopamine antagonist, which would worsen RLS symptoms.\

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body:
Always check iron levels in a patient with Restless Legs Syndrome; treat the symptoms with the dopamine agonists ropinirole or pramipexole.\

questions.reviewPearl: Typical antipsychotics block dopamine indiscriminately, which effectively treats positive symptoms (mesolimbic) but can unfortunately worsen the negative symptoms driven by the meso-cortical tract.\
questions.stem: A 20-year-old college student is brought to the psychiatrist by his roommate. He has locked himself in his room for the past 6 weeks, claiming he hears two voices having a discussion in his head telling him the President has put a "hit on him". His affect is completely flattened, and he lacks any motivation to attend classes. His negative symptoms (flattened affect, lack of motivation) are thought to be caused by a decrease of dopamine in which of the following neural pathways?
questions.educationalObjective: Correlate the negative symptoms of schizophrenia with decreased dopaminergic tone in the meso-cortical pathway.\

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body:
In the neurobiology of schizophrenia, the negative symptoms (e.g., flattened affect, alogia, avolition) are theorized to result from a 
decrease
 in dopamine activity within the prefrontal cortex / meso-cortical tract.\

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body:
The positive symptoms of schizophrenia (delusions, hallucinations) are caused by an 
excess
 of dopamine in the mesolimbic tract (A) binding to D2 receptors. The nigrostriatal tract (C) is involved in movement; blocking dopamine here causes extrapyramidal symptoms. The tuberoinfundibular tract (D) controls prolactin release; blocking dopamine here causes hyperprolactinemia.\

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Typical antipsychotics block dopamine indiscriminately, which effectively treats positive symptoms (mesolimbic) but can unfortunately worsen the negative symptoms driven by the meso-cortical tract.\
